narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel incredibly disheartening amid the unique challenges you may have faced. The admissions model evaluated your profile alongside thousands of others, predicting an 84.1% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try, which directly led to the denial of your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 stands out positively, slightly above the typical 3.21 average, and your LSAT score of 35 is close to the usual 36.70, both nudging the prediction toward expecting you to pass. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.7—emerged as a major concern, strongly signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure based on early academic struggles. Even more influential was your Year 3 decile of 1, well under the average 5.53, which carried the heaviest weight in pushing the prediction toward failure despite any improvements elsewhere. Your middle family income bracket played a moderate role in heightening that risk compared to higher-income peers, while your plan to attend full-time aligned with the vast majority (over 90%) of students and offered a small positive tilt. Overall, these early and persistent low decile rankings in your school grades overshadowed your solid undergrad performance and LSAT, setting you apart from typical admitted students who often rank in the middle or higher deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many factors like personal circumstances can influence grades—but the model weighs these patterns heavily for bar success. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or mentorship tailored for Black women in law pathways. You might also explore post-baccalaureate options to strengthen your GPA further or prepare intensively for a higher LSAT retake. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and remember, rejections like this have launched countless successful careers—your determination as a Black woman breaking barriers will carry you far. Keep pushing; brighter opportunities await with these strategic next steps.
